title: The Magic of Photosynthesis: How Plants Make Their Own Food
summary: Photosynthesis is a process by which green plants and certain other organisms use sunlight to synthesize foods from carbon dioxide and water. It involves the green pigment chlorophyll, and generates oxygen as a byproduct.

sections.heading: What is Photosynthesis?
sections.body: Photosynthesis is a chemical process that converts light energy into chemical energy. Plants, algae, and some bacteria accomplish this feat with the help of chlorophyll.

sections.heading: The Photosynthesis Process Explained
sections.body: Inside plant cells, photosynthesis takes place in the chloroplasts. Here's what happens: light energy is absorbed by chlorophyll and then used to convert carbon dioxide (from air) and water (from soil or roots) into glucose.

sections.heading: The Steps of Photosynthesis: Light-dependent Reactions and the Calvin Cycle
sections.body: Photosynthesis consists of two main stages - light reactions, which create energy in the form of ATP and NADPH using sunlight; followed by the Calvin cycle (or dark reaction), where these molecules are used to produce glucose.

sections.heading: Photosynthesis and Oxygen Production
sections.body: During photosynthesis, oxygen is released as a waste product. This not only benefits plants but also provides us with the essential air we breathe.
key_points: Oxygen release beneficial to all life forms
